planting.note: Plant in spring in moist to wet, fertile, acidic soil in full sun; pond apple is a native Florida Annona uniquely adapted to flooded, swampy, and low-lying soils where most other fruit trees would fail. It is suited to zones 10–12 and grows naturally in freshwater marshes, pond edges, and Everglades hammock margins; it tolerates seasonal flooding and salt-tolerant enough for brackish conditions.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun in zones 10–12; pond apple (Annona glabra) is a native Florida Annona that holds the distinction of being the only member of its genus adapted to flooded, swampy, and periodically inundated soils — growing naturally along the edges of ponds, marshes, and Everglades sloughs where it often forms dense thickets. The round to oval, apple-sized fruits are bland compared to other Annonaceae relatives, with a pale yellow to cream custard-like flesh that is edible but not widely prized for fresh eating; they are consumed by wildlife and occasionally used in preserves or as rootstock for grafting more flavorful Annona species such as cherimoya and soursop. Trees reach 20–40 feet at maturity and produce dense, evergreen canopies well suited to wildlife habitat, riparian buffers, and edible landscape plantings in consistently wet, low-lying sites. Pond apple requires no irrigation once established in suitable wet conditions and is tolerant of brackish water and poor, waterlogged soils.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; cherimoya is suited to zones 10–12 and sheltered zone 9b microclimates where brief cold to 25–28°F may be tolerated by established trees. Hand-pollination is strongly recommended in cultivation, as the flowers' protogynous timing and lack of natural pollinators in many regions results in poor fruit set without human intervention.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zones 10–12; cherimoya (Annona cherimola) was famously described by Mark Twain as "the most delicious fruit known to men," and it remains one of the most sought-after fruits in the world among tropical fruit enthusiasts, producing heart-shaped, scaly green fruits with a snow-white, custardy flesh of extraordinary sweetness and complexity — with flavors of pineapple, banana, vanilla, and cream all at once. Native to the Andean highlands of Ecuador and Peru, cherimoya prefers a mild, subtropical rather than hot tropical climate and performs best where summers are warm but not brutally hot, and winters are cool but frost-free. Hand-pollination is essential in cultivation: transfer pollen from freshly opened male-phase flowers to receptive female-phase flowers using a small brush or cotton swab in the morning for reliable fruit set. Grafted trees of selections such as 'Lisa,' 'Booth,' and 'McPherson' begin bearing in 2–4 years and produce superior fruit size and flavor compared to seedling trees.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; biriba is a fast-growing South American relative of cherimoya suited to zones 10–12 that begins fruiting sooner than most Annonaceae. Fruits must be harvested just before full ripeness, as they deteriorate rapidly and have a shelf life of only one to two days once ripe.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade in zones 10–12; biriba (Rollinia deliciosa, syn. Annona deliciosa) is a Brazilian tropical fruit tree in the Annonaceae family that produces large, pale yellow, spiny-looking fruits filled with a creamy, almost liquid white flesh of exceptional sweetness with a distinctive lemony-custard flavor that sets it apart from its cherimoya and sweetsop relatives. It is among the fastest-bearing of the tropical Annonaceae, often fruiting in just 2–3 years from a transplanted seedling and producing abundantly in humid tropical conditions with good moisture. The fruits are extremely perishable — ripening rapidly and becoming overripe within 24–48 hours — making them essentially a backyard-only crop unsuited to commercial shipping, which means homegrown specimens offer a flavor experience unavailable in any store. Unlike cherimoya, biriba tolerates humid tropical lowland conditions well and does not require cool nights or hand-pollination.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Java plum is a fast-growing large tree suited to zones 10–12 that can reach 30–60 feet at maturity and should be sited with ample space away from structures, pavement, and other trees. Fruit-staining from the dark purple berries can be significant, so avoid planting directly over patios or walkways.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zones 10–12; Java plum (Syzygium cumini), also called jambolan or jamun, is a large, fast-growing evergreen tree from India and Southeast Asia in the Myrtaceae family that produces dense clusters of small, oblong, deep purple to nearly black fruits with a distinctive flavor that is simultaneously sweet, tart, and astringent — with a tongue-staining purple juice widely used in wines, vinegars, syrups, and preserves across South and Southeast Asia. The astringency is due to tannins that diminish when the fruit is fully ripe; fruits are also eaten fresh with a pinch of salt, which tempers the bitterness and heightens the sweetness. Java plum has a long history in Ayurvedic medicine, with seeds, bark, and fruit used in preparations for diabetes management, digestive complaints, and other conditions. Trees are vigorous, moderately drought-tolerant once established, and remarkably adaptable — growing in a range of soil types — but produce the best fruit quality with regular moisture and full sun.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; rose apple is a medium-sized Myrtaceae tree suited to zones 10–12 that is prized for its ornamental showy white to pale yellow pompom flowers as much as for its fruit. Trees naturalize readily in humid tropical climates and are considered invasive in parts of Hawaii and other Pacific Islands — check local invasive species regulations before planting.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zones 10–12; rose apple (Syzygium jambos) is a medium-sized evergreen tree in the Myrtaceae family native to Southeast Asia that produces waxy, pale yellow to cream or blush-pink, oval fruits with a uniquely crisp, crunchy texture and a delicate, unmistakable flavor of rose water — floral, lightly sweet, and aromatic in a way unlike any other fruit. The fruits are primarily eaten fresh out of hand, and their distinctive rose fragrance also makes them popular for flavoring drinks, syrups, and desserts across Southeast Asia and the Caribbean. The tree produces spectacular ornamental flower clusters of long, creamy-white stamens resembling shaving brushes before fruit development, making it a dual-purpose landscape tree. Rose apple is adaptable, moderately fast-growing, and performs well with consistent moisture and regular balanced fertilization.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained, sandy, or rocky, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; noni is a remarkably adaptable small tree suited to zones 10–12 that tolerates poor, infertile, saline, and even volcanic soils where other trees would struggle. It is one of the fastest-bearing tropical fruit trees, often producing its first fruits within 12–18 months of transplanting.

directions: Plant in well-drained sandy, rocky, or infertile soil — including saline and coastal soils — in full sun in zones 10–12; noni (Morinda citrifolia) is a small, fast-growing evergreen tree in the Rubiaceae family native to Southeast Asia and the Pacific Islands that has been one of the most important plants in Pacific Islander traditional medicine for over 2,000 years, with virtually every part of the plant — fruit, leaves, bark, seeds, and roots — used in traditional preparations throughout Polynesia, Micronesia, and Melanesia. The distinctive, lumpy, pale greenish-white to translucent fruits have an extremely pungent, cheese-like odor when ripe that makes them challenging to consume fresh, yet the juice has become a globally marketed health supplement product worth billions of dollars annually, prized for antioxidant compounds including iridoids, scopoletin, and various polysaccharides. Trees produce fruit year-round in frost-free tropical conditions, bear their first crop remarkably quickly — within 1–2 years of transplanting — and are exceptionally drought-tolerant once established, requiring irrigation only during establishment. Noni is one of the most salt- and wind-tolerant of all tropical fruit trees, making it well suited to coastal planting in South Florida and the Gulf Coast.
